01_initial_request.input_data: Physiological signals from wearable sensors collected under free-living conditions from a large-scale population
01_initial_request.user_intent: To enable accurate cardiorespiratory fitness assessment without the costs and risks of direct exhaustive exercise testing
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Achieve prediction performance superior to traditional and state-of-the-art non-exercise models

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) is a strong predictor of cardiovascular disease risk but direct VO2max measurement requires costly laboratory equipment. [Gap] Need to estimate VO2max from free-living wearable sensor data without exercise testing. [Decision] To achieve accurate fitness estimation, chose to use Actiheart wearable sensors to collect heart rate and movement data on participants for 6 complete days, expecting to obtain continuous physiological signals for model training.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Collected heart rate and accelerometer data from 11,059 participants with concurrent laboratory VO2max measurements from submaximal treadmill tests

references.citation: Brage, S. et al. Reliability and validity of the combined heart rate and movement sensor actiheart. Eur. J. Clin. Nutr. 59, 561–570 (2005)
references.relevance: Validates the Actiheart sensor for measuring heart rate and physical activity

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Raw sensor data requires preprocessing to extract meaningful features for machine learning. [Gap] Need to convert raw time series into structured features suitable for model input. [Decision] To achieve feature extraction, chose to use Pandas and Numpy libraries to extract 68 statistical features from sensor time series, expecting to obtain comprehensive feature vectors covering distribution attributes.

parameters.time_series_inputs: raw acceleration, HR, HRV, Acceleration-derived Euclidean Norm Minus One, Acceleration-derived Metabolic Equivalents
parameters.statistics_extracted: mean, minimum, maximum, standard deviation, percentiles (25%, 50%, 75%), slope of linear regression fit
parameters.temporal_encoding: cyclical temporal features for month of year as (x,y) coordinates on a circle

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Derived 68 comprehensive features from sensor time series covering diverse distribution attributes

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] High-dimensional feature space may contain redundancy and noise. [Gap] Need to reduce dimensionality while preserving variance. [Decision] To achieve dimensionality reduction, chose to use Principal Components Analysis (PCA) to reduce features from 68 to 48, expecting to retain 99.99% of variance while reducing computational complexity.

parameters.application: applied to training data, saved projection applied to test set to avoid information leakage

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Reduced feature dimensionality from 68 to 48 components while retaining 99.99% of variance

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Features need normalization for neural network training. [Gap] Need to standardize feature scales before model training. [Decision] To achieve feature normalization, chose to use standard scaling to remove mean and scale to unit variance, expecting to obtain normalized features suitable for deep learning.

parameters.scaling_method: standard scaling (removing the mean and scaling to unit variance)
parameters.application: fitted on training data, applied individually to test set to avoid information leakage

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Normalized training and test data with consistent scaling parameters

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Need to establish baseline performance for comparison with deep learning models. [Gap] Traditional non-exercise models show limited validity (R² of 0.35-0.61). [Decision] To achieve baseline comparison, chose to use linear regression models with different feature combinations, expecting to obtain reference performance metrics for model comparison.

parameters.input_combinations: anthropometry alone, RHR alone, anthropometry + RHR, comprehensive model with sensors + RHR + anthropometry
parameters.training_set: n=8384 participants with baseline data only
parameters.test_set: n=2675 participants with baseline and followup data

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Linear regression achieved R² of 0.35-0.37 with single variables, R² of 0.61 when combined, R² of 0.67 with comprehensive model

references.citation: Uth, N. et al. Estimation of vo2max from the ratio between hrmax and hrnest–the heart rate ratio method. Eur. J. Appl. Physiol. 91, 111–115 (2004)
references.relevance: Traditional non-exercise prediction equation for comparison

references.citation: Tanaka, H. et al. Age-predicted maximal heart rate revisited. J. Am. Coll. Cardiol. 37, 153–156 (2001)
references.relevance: Age-predicted maximal heart rate equation used in traditional models

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Linear models may not capture complex non-linear relationships in physiological data. [Gap] Need more expressive model architecture for better prediction accuracy. [Decision] To achieve improved prediction performance, chose to use deep neural network with Tensorflow/Keras with 2 densely-connected feed-forward layers, expecting to obtain superior correlation with ground truth VO2max.

parameters.architecture: two densely-connected feed-forward layers with 128 units each
parameters.early_stopping: after 15 epochs when validation loss stops improving

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Deep neural network achieved correlation r=0.82 (95% CI 0.80-0.83) with ground truth VO2max in holdout sample, R² of 0.67

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Model needs external validation on independent longitudinal cohort. [Gap] Need to assess model generalizability to future timepoints. [Decision] To achieve longitudinal validation, chose to apply trained model from Task 1 to Fenland II participants (7 years later), expecting to obtain fitness predictions for future timepoint.

parameters.model: trained model from Task 1 used in inference mode
parameters.matched_population: N=2042 participants with sensor data for both cohorts

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Model achieved r=0.84 for VO2max future prediction and r=0.82 for VO2max current prediction with new sensor data from future week

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Need to assess model's ability to detect fitness change over time. [Gap] Delta prediction shows moderate predictability with correlation of 0.23 for regression. [Decision] To achieve change detection, chose to reformulate as classification task predicting direction of fitness change, expecting to obtain AUC metrics for change prediction.

parameters.architecture: smaller network with one Dense layer of 128 units and sigmoid output

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Achieved AUC of 0.61 for direction prediction, AUC of 0.72 for substantial change, AUC of 0.74 for dramatic change

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Need to understand model's latent representations for patient subtyping. [Gap] Linear dimension-reduction techniques cannot reveal learned feature representations. [Decision] To achieve latent space visualization, chose to use t-SNE nonlinear dimension-reduction on penultimate layer activations, expecting to obtain visual clustering by VO2max levels.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Latent space showed VO2max clustering inversely related and more apparent compared to original feature space, enabling fitness-aware patient subtyping

references.citation: Van der Maaten, L. & Hinton, G. Visualizing data using t-sne. J. Mach. Learn. Res. 9 (2008)
references.relevance: t-SNE algorithm for dimensionality reduction and visualization

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Need external validation with direct maximal VO2max testing (gold standard). [Gap] Fenland study used submaximal tests; need validation against true maximal testing. [Decision] To achieve gold standard validation, chose to use UK Biobank Validation Study participants with direct maximal exercise testing, expecting to obtain external validation metrics.

parameters.test_type: independent maximal exercise test completed to exhaustion

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Model overpredicted with mean VO2max=39.9 vs actual 32.9±7 (RMSE=8.998) due to distribution shift; after matching fitness statistics, RMSE=5.19

references.citation: Gonzales, T. I. et al. Descriptive epidemiology of cardiorespiratory fitness in UK adults: The fenland study. medRxiv (2022)
references.relevance: UK Biobank Validation Study cohort description

03_success_verification.validation_technique: External validation on holdout test set (Fenland I), longitudinal validation on Fenland II cohort (7 years later), and gold standard validation on UK Biobank Validation Study with direct maximal VO2max testing

correlation_holdout.interpretation: Strong correlation between predicted and true VO2max in holdout sample with 95% CI 0.80-0.83

r_squared.interpretation: Best performance attained using deep neural network combining wearable sensors, RHR, and anthropometric data

longitudinal_correlation.interpretation: Correlation for future VO2max prediction using baseline data only

delta_correlation.interpretation: Correlation between delta of predictions and ground truth fitness change over 7 years (p<0.005)

classification_auc_direction.interpretation: Performance in predicting direction of fitness change (N=2675)

classification_auc_substantial.interpretation: Performance in distinguishing substantial fitness change (80%/20% quantiles, N=1068)

classification_auc_dramatic.interpretation: Performance in distinguishing dramatic fitness change (90%/10% quantiles, N=535)

mae_subgroups.interpretation: MAE across all subgroups (sex, age, weight, BMI, height) indicating acceptable accuracy for population-scale use

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. The deep learning model combining wearable sensor data with anthropometric measures achieved correlation r=0.82 with ground truth VO2max, outperforming traditional non-exercise models (R² 0.67 vs 0.35-0.61 for linear models). The model demonstrated adaptability over time with strong performance on longitudinal data (7 years later) and successfully detected fitness changes. Results show wearables can enable accurate cardiorespiratory fitness assessment without costly laboratory exercise testing, meeting the quantifiable goal of superior prediction performance compared to traditional and state-of-the-art non-exercise models.
